The Insurance Coverage Summary outlines corporate liability, cyber risk, and physical asset insurance shields maintained by Vodafone Idea Limited (VIL) for VI Business (India) enterprise operations.
Covers financial losses strictly from proven failure of VI Business to perform telecom/IT services as defined in signed BoM and SLA.
Excludes coverage for breaches originating from customer's own insecure LAN or compromised edge endpoints.
Workmen’s Compensation adheres strictly to the Workmen’s Compensation Act, 1923 (India).
Managed SD-WAN & MPLS are covered by E&O for configuration faults and CGL for physical installation risks.
Security Operations (SOC) are exclusively covered under Cyber Liability for missed IOCs leading to proven breaches, subject to forensic limitations.
Raw Bandwidth (Unmanaged ILL) excludes E&O coverage; SLA penalty (Service Credits) is the sole remedy for downtime.
Customers must be provided proof of policies within 15 days of contract signature.
Adding individual Indian enterprises as 'Additional Insured' on master VIL corporate policies is prohibited.
If an RFP mandates adding an Indian enterprise as 'Additional Insured,' Legal will offer a 'Waiver of Subrogation' clause in the MSA instead.
The Compliance Matrix in Section 4 must be used to answer questions about insurance limits.
If an RFP demands 'INR 500 Crores of CGL,' a legal clarification must be filed stating VI Business adheres to a corporate AOY limit of INR 250 Crores.
"Unsupported/Best Effort" configurations disclaim liability for performance degradation or outages.
Interoperability is only guaranteed on OEM firmware versions explicitly approved by the VI Business Change Advisory Board (CAB).
Customers forcefully demanding unvetted firmware upgrades instantly void hardware SLA guarantees.
Cite Section 7 to defensively reject RFP requirements implying VI Business is responsible for integrating completely bespoke or sunset third-party applications.
The Legal Clause Response Playbook applies exclusively to Enterprise B2B agreements under Indian jurisdiction.

acceptable_template: If the customer pushes back, VI Business's total aggregate liability shall not exceed 100% of the Total Contract Value (TCV) of the underlying Service Order.
Uncapped liability must never be accepted except for statutory carve-outs (e.g., bodily injury, gross negligence, willful misconduct).
SLA penalties for minor outages must be paid as Service Credits, not cash, and represent 100% of the Monthly Recurring Charge (MRC).
Service Credits are the Customer’s sole and exclusive remedy for performance failures under the SLA.
Service Credits are capped at a maximum of 30% of the MRC for the affected link/site in the billing month of failure.
Termination for convenience requires 90 days’ prior written notice, with Early Termination Charges (ETC) equal to 100% of unamortized CapEx and 50% of remaining MRC for the unexpired term.
Customer may conduct paper-based audits of VI Business’s ISO 27001 and SOC 2 compliance reports on demand.
Physical on-site audits require 30 days’ prior notice, are limited to once per calendar year, and must be strictly escorted.
Clauses requiring VI Business to act as a 'Data Fiduciary' for unencrypted Customer Payload must be rejected; VI Business acts solely as a 'Data Processor'.
Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations must conduct a bridging review to ensure compliance with Indian DoT licensing rules when enforcing a Global MSA.
The Monthly SLA Performance & Attainment Report must include calculated uptime percentage per circuit, MTTR, MTTRes, and penalty/service credits for breaches.
Direct SQL access to the underlying VI databases is strictly prohibited under the Information Security Policy.
SIP Trunking (Voice) requires a VI Business dedicated fiber/copper underlay.
VoIP over Open Internet (Toll Bypass) architectures are prohibited as they violate DoT OSP guidelines.
Customer contracts for exceptions must include a 'Limitations of Supportability' annexure outlining compromised SLAs.
All commercial quotes must legally bind pricing to a specific baseline physical and operational reality, with any post-signature changes triggering the formal Change Request (CR) process.
If site feasibility changes (e.g., rural vs. urban building permission), core OTC assumptions fail, requiring immediate commercial revision before hardware ordering.
The QMF strictly aligns with ITIL v4 Release Management best practices.
A random 10% sampling of all completed 'Standard Change' tickets must be audited monthly by the PMO to ensure compliance with structural approvals.
Centralized Next-Gen Firewall (NGFW) clusters must be deployed at the VI Mumbai core for BFSI security.
SSL deep-packet inspection (DPI) must be mandatory for BFSI security overlays.
All BFSI data must reside within India to comply with Reserve Bank of India (RBI) mandates.
BFSI deployments must meet a 6-hour disaster recovery Recovery Time Objective (RTO) via the Pune DR site.
Regulatory/Compliance risks include changes to DoT (Department of Telecommunications) OSP routing rules.
DPDP Act 2023 breach fines
Risks are mathematically scored using a standardized 5x5 Probability vs. Impact matrix
Score range 1-6: Low (Green) - Accept Risk. Monitor quarterly
Score range 8-12: Medium (Amber) - Mitigate. Implement controls within 90 days
Score range 15-20: High (Red) - Mitigate Immediately. Weekly C-Suite review
Score range 25: Critical (Black) - Cease Operations. Immediate CEO intervention
VI Business assumes zero liability for risks introduced by customer-mandated architecture against engineering advice (e.g., single-point-of-failure firewall).
Digital evidence must comply with the Indian IT Act 2000 to survive scrutiny in Indian courts of law.
Under CERT-In April 2022 directives, VI Business must report severe cyber incidents (e.g., ransomware, DDoS) to the national agency within 6 hours of detection, overriding any NDAs.
When responding to RFP 'Incident Response' matrices, include Section 3 (Classification) and Section 7 (Reporting Obligations) to demonstrate CERT-In compliance.

The SDD is the cornerstone of the Master Services Agreement (MSA).
Enterprise customers explicitly agree to the parameters defined within the SDD when signing a contract.
The Service Description supersedes all preliminary marketing brochures, emails, or verbal commitments made during the pre-sales cycle.
The Demarcation Point is the absolute legal boundary of VI Business's liability.
VI Business is responsible for everything from the public internet/MPLS cloud up to, and including, the physical Gigabit Ethernet port on the VI-owned core/edge router.
Failure by the customer to adhere to responsibilities absolves VI Business of SLA penalty liability.
Physical edge CPE (Cisco/Fortinet routers) remains VI Business property after 60-month contract term unless a formal "Hardware Buy-Out" clause exists.
Use Section 7 (Demarcation) to reject contract modifications attempting to include free internal LAN support.
The Customer holds ultimate legal leverage to compel the incumbent’s cooperation during transitions.
